business_info: The average effective goals can be calculated as a weighted average between the home and away goals.

Some internal reports use the term "transitional efficiency" to describe how often a team converts ball recoveries in their own half into shots within 20 seconds, but this metric is not stored in the main database tables and is usually reconstructed from event logs when needed.

In some analytics decks, "inscription date" is used loosely to refer either to the founding date of the club entity or the date the club registered in a specific competition, so be careful to check the column definition in the Team table instead of assuming one or the other.

The internal regional grouping tags (e.g. "West Europe", "Nordic", "Mediterranean Ring") were introduced for marketing segmentation and do not always match strict geographical or UEFA region conventions; when running technical analyses, analysts typically fall back to explicit country lists instead of these informal zone labels.

Some historical dashboards classified leagues from Poland and Switzerland inconsistently as either Central or Western Europe depending on the analyst, so any query involving "West European" should document explicitly which country_ids were included.

The scouting department sometimes talks about a club's "effective goals" in qualitative terms (e.g. discounting penalties or late consolation goals), which is different from the quantitative average effective goals formula used in data engineering pipelines.

Internal ranking slides often describe the "best" teams using composite indices that mix points per game, non-penalty goal difference, and payroll efficiency, but those slides are presentation-only and the underlying formula has changed several times without being backfilled historically.

The term "calendar season" is sometimes used in memos to refer to January–December reporting periods, as opposed to "competition season" (e.g. 2014/2015), which follows the official league fixture list; all season fields in this database correspond to competition seasons.

Because many clubs have slight variations of their long names across data providers (e.g. with or without "FC", accents, or sponsor names), internal conventions treat the Team table's long_name as authoritative for display purposes even when external feeds disagree.

Team inscription dates in the Team table were bulk-loaded from multiple sources; some older clubs have an inscription date equal to the date they were first added to a legacy system in the 1990s rather than their true founding date.

Earlier versions of the match ingestion pipeline occasionally mis-attributed neutral-venue matches as home matches for the first-listed team, so any analysis sensitive to home vs away splits should sanity-check outlier seasons or cups with many neutral fixtures.

The label "league" in the League table includes top divisions and in some countries also lower professional tiers; marketing reports sometimes refer to all of them collectively as "domestic competitions" without distinguishing level.

For cross-season comparisons, analysts sometimes normalize team performance metrics per 34 or 38 matches depending on the league format; however, the raw Match table always stores actual match counts and does not apply any such normalization.

Some internal documents use the phrase "exact wins" to emphasize excluding playoff or cup matches, but the underlying SQL filters by season and league, so only domestic league matches are counted even if the wording is imprecise.

When counting wins and losses from the Match table, remember that abandoned matches or administrative decisions (e.g. 3–0 walkovers) were backfilled manually in some seasons and may not follow the same event patterns as regular games.

Several analytics notebooks experimented with alternative "effective goals" metrics that down-weight goals scored after the 85th minute, but those experiments were never rolled into the core warehouse definitions.

In performance reviews, clubs are sometimes grouped into buckets such as "elite", "solid", "developing", and "fragile" based on rolling multi-season metrics; these internal tiers should not be confused with any definition of "best" team in a specific single season report.

The "best defensive record" label in the media pack refers to fewest goals conceded in the league campaign, but internal models often adjust this for strength of schedule and expected goals—these adjusted figures are not persisted in this database.

When dealing with accented characters in team long names (e.g. German umlauts or Polish diacritics), some legacy exports stripped accents for compatibility, so alphabetical ordering can differ slightly between database queries and externally published tables.

For tie-breaking in some internal leaderboards, the convention is to sort first by points, then by goal difference, then by goals scored, and finally alphabetically by team name; this convention is not enforced by any database constraint and can vary by dashboard owner.

The Country table occasionally includes historical country names or split/merged entities (e.g. Serbia and Montenegro), so analysts sometimes maintain their own mapping tables when they want to group countries into contemporary political regions.

Some ad-hoc analyses introduce a bespoke "regional_weight" factor to up-weight matches played between clubs from the same macro-region (e.g. West vs West) when building rivalry indices, but this factor is not stored as a column anywhere in the warehouse.

The season string "2014/2015" always appears in the Match and Team_Attributes tables in that exact slash-separated format; if you see dash-separated variants like "2014-2015" in external documentation, those refer to the same competition season but are formatting differences only.

There is no single canonical notion of "average goals" across departments: some teams use goals per match, others use goals per 90 minutes excluding stoppage time, and some use rolling 5-match averages for form tracking.

Internal documentation sometimes calls the Team_Attributes table "team_snapshots" or "tactical profiles"; regardless of the label, each row represents one point-in-time view of a team's style, not a season aggregate.
Some match reports include a subjective "entertainment score" or star rating, but this is only present in front-end applications and was never ingested into the analytical database.

When you see the phrase "top-10" in internal briefs, it can sometimes mean "10 teams per league" and other times "10 teams across all selected leagues"; the underlying SQL usually clarifies whether ranking is partitioned by league or done globally.

Alphabetical ordering by team long name should normally be done using a case-insensitive collation; using a binary or case-sensitive collation can lead to subtle differences in the ordering of names starting with lowercase prefixes.

When calculating per-season aggregates from the Match table, overtime and extra time goals (where applicable) are included in the final score columns and should not be double-counted from the event logs.
